In Turin, however, one dazzlingly impish skater has captured attention previously given to other, better known characters.
American figure skater Johnny Weir now sits in second place behind Russia's Evgeni Plushenko.
Weir is looking forward to a possible quadruple jump tomorrow night, something that he has never done in competition.
Sports commentator Geoff Calkins profiled Weir for the Commercial Appeal, his newspaper in Memphis, and he joins us now from Turin to tell us about Weir and Camille?
Who is Camille, Geoff Calkins?
Well Neal, I'm stunned.
You know, you're just setting me up, because everybody knows Camille by now.
Camille is Johnny Weir's single red glove that he wears.
He named his glove?
He named his glove, yes.
Camille is the name of his glove.
In fact, after he finished his short program the other day, he, come to what's called the mix-zone, where you mix with the unwashed reporters, and he warned us, he said, Camille is coming off.
And sure enough, off came Camille.
So it was quite the show.
And Johnny Weir is absolutely a delight, really.
He is, it's hard to describe, but he is certainly, what you see is what you get in Johnny.
I mentioned Evgeni Plushenko.
He put on a performance yesterday, it got more points, it's a new scoring system, granted, but he got more points than anybody has ever gotten in a short program.
And the guy who finishes second said, he's going to win unless he falls down three times.
Yeah.
Johnny, Johnny Weir was very clear that the only way that Plushenko can lose this is if he falls three times, and then he said, someone might, might squeeze by him and win by a point.
He is not unlike Roger Federer right now, in tennis.
He is playing a different sort of game, a game with which the others are unfamiliar.
And so it's pretty much conceded that this is a fight for second place.
And whether that'll be Johnny or not will be interesting to see.
And he is, is he emerging as America's sweetheart?
Well, that's the great question.
With Michelle Kwan gone, can Johnny Weir be America's sweetheart?
He makes no pretenses that that's what he'd like to be.
He calls himself Tinkerbell.
He has two Chihuahuas, one of which is named Bon Bon.

When he travels, he actually came into the village here and really, if you're going to be an athlete at the Olympics, you get extra points, extra sort of PR points if you stay at the village, but he said, he said I'm very princessey when it comes to travel, and I'm not a village person.
It's dusty, under-decorated and the beds are not very soft.
I hate carrying my own luggage and I hate trekking upstairs.
So he's not enamored of the village, and I think he may be the first male athlete in the history of the Olympics to call himself princessey.
And, he may not be a village person, he certainly sounds like a village person.
Yes.
What you said.
Okay.

That's the funny thing about him.

Until he got into the pros, but go ahead.
Until he got to the pros when he became famously less tough, yeah, when he was run over.

Well, I went to basketball games with her.
I went to Dodger games with her.
She was not one who wanted to do the press.
Her famous line is, it's only a movie.
So as a publicist, a young publicist, what was your responsibility, what were you trying to do?
The main job was to keep people at bay with Doris.
You didn't want to expose her to situations where she was not in control.
My favorite Doris Day story is - see, I was young, and Life magazine was hugely important.
And they wanted to do a tribute to Doris Day on her 40th birthday.
So I got very excited.
I went to Doris and I said, you know, we had this great opportunity.
They want to do this tribute to you.
And it's just very exciting because it's guaranteed.
And she said, no, I don't think so.
Why would I do it?
I said, well, because you're number one.
She said, then what's the point?
And I was sort of stumped.
And I said, well, would you do it as a favor to me?
She said, no.
That was pretty audacious.
She said, but let's go see the Lakers tonight.
So she didn't do it.
How did you get the likes of - well, Jodie Foster, Al Pacino, Goldie Hawn, Demi Moore; these really big stars were your clients by the early '70s.
Well, when the first company that I had was in partnership with two other women and it was called Pickwick, and we only had like, 15 clients, but they were all A clients.
There were huge stars.
But why did they pick you?
I mean, what were you doing for them?
Because they presumably could have had - they could have anybody as a publicist.
What were you bringing to them that made them choose you?
Most of the publicists in those days were men.
And women are exceptional at this business because we're very nurturing; people feel like they can talk to women better than men.
We're better at taking care of their personal problems.
And I don't mean, you know, their marriages and so forth, I'm talking about their relationships with the studios and relationships with the press.
And the press liked dealing with women.
Hmm.
It might be said that your greatest triumph in shaping an image was your longtime client Tom Cruise.
He was a superstar, but all of the details of his life that we know so much about now, very little of that type of thing was known about Tom Cruise.
What was known about him was, in a sense, what you and he wanted to be known about him.
You know, you want them to know what you want them to know, and the rest is your private information.
But that isn't to say over those years that everyone could have pulled it off.
You - I think you enjoyed that you were called Dr. No.
It was cute.
And...
I mean, it didn't bother me.
You know, if you were...
That came from, you told people no. You're not going to...
If you were extraordinarily sensitive, you shouldn't have the kind of job that I had.
But this was a difficult moment because he actually walked away from you.

Fourteen, fifteen years?
Fourteen.
